sections.subtitle: Current Form
sections.text: Brighton & Hove Albion have shown mixed results in their last 5 matches, with 2 wins, 2 draws, and 1 loss, displaying resilience but struggling with consistency. Everton, also with mixed form, have 1 win, 3 draws, and 1 loss in their last 5, indicating a defensively solid but offensively limited approach, with most games under 2.5 goals. Both teams have had issues keeping clean sheets recently, with Brighton failing to do so in their last 4 matches and Everton conceding in 4 of their last 5.

sections.subtitle: Head-to-Head History
sections.text: In the last two head-to-head encounters, Everton have had the upper hand, winning both matches (2-0 at home and 1-0 away). These games have been low-scoring, with under 2.5 goals in recent meetings, and Everton often scoring first, as seen in 5 of their last 6 matchups against Brighton. Disciplinary issues are also common in these fixtures, with over 4.5 cards in 5 of the last 6 games.

sections.subtitle: Season Statistics
sections.text: Brighton, sitting 12th with 30 points, have scored 33 goals (9th in the league) but conceded 31, showing defensive vulnerabilities. Everton, in 10th with 33 points, have a more conservative attack with only 25 goals but a slightly better defense, conceding 26. Brighton’s higher possession (52.5%) and pressing intensity (top in final third possessions won) contrast with Everton’s efficiency in limited chances.

sections.subtitle: Key Players and Tactics
sections.text: Brighton’s Danny Welbeck (8 goals, 5th in the league) is a major threat upfront, while Jan Paul van Hecke adds defensive stability with a 7.35 rating. Everton rely on Thierno Barry (5 goals) and Iliman Ndiaye (4 goals, 2 assists) for attacking output, with James Garner in form (7.82 average rating). Brighton’s possession-based style may dominate, but Everton’s counter-attacking ability could exploit defensive lapses.

sections.subtitle: Match Outlook
sections.text: Given Sofascore’s prediction of a 38% chance for a Brighton win, 31% for a draw, and 31% for an Everton win, this match appears evenly poised. Brighton’s home advantage and attacking potential slightly favor them, but Everton’s recent head-to-head dominance and defensive resilience suggest a tight contest. Expect a low-scoring affair with potential for cards and limited corners based on historical trends.

halftimeSections.subtitle: Half-time overview
halftimeSections.text: The first half at the American Express Stadium between Brighton & Hove Albion and Everton ended in a goalless draw. Brighton dominated possession and created more attacking opportunities, showing intent to break the deadlock. Everton, on the other hand, struggled to generate meaningful chances and seemed content to absorb pressure while looking for counter-attacking openings. Both teams displayed resilience in defense, with no goals conceded despite some promising moments for the home side.

halftimeSections.subtitle: Brighton's attacking intent
halftimeSections.text: Brighton showed clear dominance in the attacking third during the first half. They frequently entered the final third and penalty area, creating multiple big chances that went unconverted. Their attacking play was marked by several spells of sustained pressure, particularly in the early stages and towards the end of the half. Players like Danny Welbeck and Kaoru Mitoma were at the forefront of these efforts, but their inability to finish has kept the scoreline level. Brighton will need to capitalize on these opportunities in the second half to secure the lead.

halftimeSections.subtitle: Everton's defensive resilience
halftimeSections.text: Everton adopted a more cautious approach in the first half, focusing on defensive solidity. They managed to limit Brighton's effectiveness in front of goal despite the home side's territorial advantage. Their defense, anchored by players like Michael Keane and Jarrad Branthwaite, held firm under pressure, with several key clearances preventing dangerous situations. However, their lack of attacking output means they have been unable to test Brighton's defense significantly. Everton will likely look to maintain this structure while seeking rare chances to strike on the break.

halftimeSections.subtitle: Attack momentum shifts
halftimeSections.text: The first half saw Brighton largely dictating the pace with consistent attacking momentum, especially during the opening minutes and in key periods midway through the half. They enjoyed prolonged spells of dominance, pushing forward with intent and creating waves of pressure. Everton had brief moments of momentum, particularly around the middle of the half, where they managed to shift play into Brighton's half, but these spells were short-lived and lacked the cutting edge needed to create real danger. Brighton regained control towards the latter stages, finishing the half as the more threatening side.

halftimeSections.subtitle: Key players to watch
halftimeSections.text: For Brighton, Danny Welbeck stands out as a potential game-changer in the second half, having been heavily involved in their attacking play and coming close to scoring. His ability to convert chances will be crucial. Kaoru Mitoma also showed promise with his runs and creativity on the wing. For Everton, Jordan Pickford's performance in goal has been vital in keeping the scoreline level, and his composure will be tested further. Iliman Ndiaye could be a player to watch if Everton manage to exploit counter-attacks, though he needs more support from midfield.

halftimeSections.subtitle: Second half outlook
halftimeSections.text: Brighton are likely to continue their aggressive approach in the second half, leveraging their home advantage and superior possession to push for a goal. Their missed chances in the first half suggest they have the potential to score if they maintain focus. Everton, meanwhile, may stick to their defensive setup, hoping to frustrate Brighton and capitalize on any lapses through quick transitions. The game could hinge on whether Brighton can finally break through or if Everton can steal a goal against the run of play. A tactical adjustment from either manager could shift the balance in what promises to be a tighter second half.
